title: Psalms 43
subtitle: Send Me Your Light and Your Truth

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Longing for the Temple

heb.paragraph: "Send me your light (ʾōr) and your faithful care, let them lead me" (v.3). Psalm 43 is the companion to Psalm 42 (possibly originally one psalm — they share the refrain). The psalmist asks for vindication against an ungodly nation and for light and truth to guide him back to God’s dwelling.

notes.note: "Vindicate me, my God, and plead my cause. You are God my stronghold — why have you rejected me?" The "why" questions continue from Psalm 42. The exiled psalmist feels both attacked by enemies and abandoned by God. MacArthur emphasizes the doctrinal significance of this passage within the framework of systematic theology. The text establishes principles that remain normative for the church — not merely descriptive of ancient Israel but prescriptive for how God's people should understand His character and respond in obedience.

refs.note: Jesus as the light of the world fulfills the psalmist's prayer for God to send his light.
Jesus as 'the truth' answers the plea for God's truth to guide and bring the worshiper to his dwelling.
Coming to Mount Zion and the heavenly Jerusalem completes the psalm's journey to God's holy mountain.

hist.context: Psalm 43 lacks a superscription, unusual in Book II, supporting its original unity with Psalm 42. The 'ungodly nation' and 'deceitful and wicked' enemies may refer to non-Israelite oppressors in the northern exile context. The prayer for vindication (Hebrew: shāphaṭ) invokes God as judge between the righteous and wicked.

sections.header: Verses 3–5 — “Then I Will Go to the Altar of God”

heb.paragraph: "Then I will go to the altar (mizbēaḥ) of God, to God, my joy and my delight. I will praise you with the lyre, O God, my God" (v.4). The destination of the guided journey: the altar, which means God’s presence. The refrain returns one final time: "Why, my soul, are you downcast? Put your hope in God" (v.5). Third and last occurrence. The exile psalm trilogy (42–43) ends in hope, not resolution.

notes.note: "Send me your light and your faithful care, let them lead me; let them bring me to your holy mountain." Light and truth are personified guides leading the exile home. The destination: the altar of God, who is "my joy and my delight."
"Why, my soul, are you downcast?" Third refrain. The exile continues, but so does the hope. The psalm ends in trust, not triumph.

refs.note: Drawing near to God with confidence through Jesus completes the psalmist's longing to approach the altar.
Serving before God's throne day and night, with the Lamb as shepherd, fulfills the temple-longing theme.
Rejoicing in the Lord always echoes the psalmist's resolve to praise despite circumstances.

hist.context: The altar and lyre represent the full restoration of worship the psalmist craves. 'God my exceeding joy' (literally 'God of the gladness of my rejoicing') expresses the intensity of temple worship as the source of life's deepest pleasure. The final refrain commands the soul to hope, modeling the discipline of directing emotions toward truth.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: "Vindicate me, my God, and plead my cause…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVYou are God my stronghold. Why have you rejected me? Why must I go about mourning, oppressed by the enemy?ESVFor you are the God in whom I take refuge; why have you rejected me? Why do I go about mourning because of the oppression of the enemy?NIVSend me your light and your faithful care, let them lead me; let them bring me to your holy mountain, to the place where you dwell.ESVSend out your light and your truth; let them lead me; let them bring me to your holy hill and to your dwelling!

rows.label: The Plea — 43:1–2
rows.text: "Vindicate me." Attacked and abandoned. "Why have you rejected me?"

hebtext.note: "Send me your light (ʾōr) and your faithful care, let them lead me" (v.3). Psalm 43 is the companion to Psalm 42 (possibly originally one psalm — they share the refrain). The psalmist asks for vindication against an ungodly nation and for light and truth to guide him back to God’s dwelling.

"Then I will go to the altar (mizbēaḥ) of God, to God, my joy and my delight. I will praise you with the lyre, O God, my God" (v.4). The destination of the guided journey: the altar, which means God’s presence. The refrain returns one final time: "Why, my soul, are you downcast? Put your hope in God" (v.5). Third and last occurrence. The exile psalm trilogy (42–43) ends in hope, not resolution.

themes.note: Faith: the refrain’s final occurrence — hope persists through exile. Worship: the altar as destination and joy.

prayer_prompt: As you consider 'Send me your light and your faithful care,' consider asking God whether you are waiting for His guidance to find you or actively asking Him to send it — and whether you would follow it if it led somewhere uncomfortable.
